turns.content: I'm trying to set up fuzz testing for my HTTP parser in Rust, but I’m having trouble wrapping my head around how to do it. Can you help me get started?

Absolutely! So, you want to create a cargo-fuzz target to test your HTTP parser? That’s a great choice — fuzzing helps find edge cases and crashes that unit tests might miss. Have you heard of the `cargo-fuzz` crate?
Yes, but I’m confused about how it works with HTTP parsers. Should I start from scratch or wrap an existing parser like hyper's?

Good question! If you’re building your own parser, creating a `cargo-fuzz` target is straightforward: just define the fuzz function that takes bytes as input and runs your parser. But if you’re integrating with an existing crate like `hyper`, it makes sense to leverage their existing API in the fuzz target. This way, you can gradually improve confidence without rewriting everything. It’s like upgrading a car engine — sometimes it’s easier to swap parts than rebuild the whole thing.
Hey, I'm having a tough time with fuzz testing an HTTP parser in Rust. When I run cargo-fuzz on my target function, it crashes on some inputs, but I can't reproduce them locally. What am I missing?

Ah, that's a classic issue when fuzzing: reproducing crashes isn't always straightforward. Have you tried collecting the corpus seeds cargo-fuzz generates? They're usually found in the `target/debug/fuzz-corpus` directory.
Hmm, I didn't realize that. I thought if a fuzz test crashes during CI, just rerunning locally should give me the same crash. Is there anything else?

Good question! The fuzzing process uses random mutations and coverage-guided exploration to discover inputs. So even though the input might be large or malformed, cargo-fuzz keeps track of which seeds led to crashes. You can load those corpus files into your local fuzz target with `-seed` options.
I see. But what if I miss the right seed? Should I just rerun the whole fuzz process locally?
Avoid that! Instead, you can specify a subset of corpus files with `-seed` like this: `cargo fuzz run my_fuzz_target -seed=corpus_file`. This helps narrow down to inputs most likely causing crashes.
Okay, so I should load the seed that caused the crash in CI into my local fuzz target?
Yes! That will make your local runs much more reproducible and give you a clear starting point. You'll likely get the same or similar failures, which speeds up debugging.
